elements.sent-2411-001: People often use visualizations not only to explore a dataset but also to draw generalizable conclusions about underlying models or phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-002: While previous research has viewed deviations from rational analysis as problematic, we hypothesize that human reliance on non-normative heuristics may be advantageous in certain situations.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this study, we investigate scenarios where human intuition might outperform idealized statistical rationality.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our experiment assesses participants' accuracy in characterizing the parameters of known data-generating models from bivariate visualizations.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our findings show that, while participants generally demonstrated lower accuracy than statistical models, they often outperformed Bayesian agents, particularly when dealing with extreme samples.
elements.sent-2411-006: These results suggest that, even when deviating from rationality, human gut reactions to visualizations can provide an advantage.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our findings offer insights into how analyst intuition and statistical models can be integrated to improve inference and decision-making, with important implications for the design of visual analytics tools.
elements.sent-2411-008: Visualization tools play an increasingly important role in data analysis and decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-009: With recent advances in LLM applications, people can quickly generate visualizations even without having an analytical background.
elements.sent-2411-010: One significant impact of those advanced AI tools such as ChatGPT on data visualization is their ability to enable conversational queries and commands.
elements.sent-2411-011: Users can interact with visualizations using plain language, asking questions, or requesting specific visualizations.
elements.sent-2411-012: There has also been a growing number of proposals for visualization-specific tools that utilize LLMs as their core component \cite{maddigan2023chat2vis, wang2023llm4vis, li2024prompt4vis .}
elements.sent-2411-013: While such advanced tools provide assistance in seeking insights from data \cite{zhao2024leva, wang2024scientific , making accurate inferences from visualizations can still be challenging, as it requires one to carefully account for potential uncertainties and variabilities in the data.}
elements.sent-2411-014: For example, the analyst might overinterpret a visualization displaying an unusual or outlying sample, causing them to infer spurious features that do not exist in the data-generating process (i.e., false discovery).
elements.sent-2411-015: Conversely, they may fail to sufficiently consider data that is in front of them, and instead fall back to their prior belief --- a potential manifestation of confirmation bias.
elements.sent-2411-016: We are motivated by the idea of collaborative exploration and interpretation of visualizations, where human cognition works alongside AI systems to derive insights from complex datasets.
elements.sent-2411-017: Human analysts often bring to bear their own hunches when interpreting visualizations.
elements.sent-2411-018: In coming to an inference, the analyst might choose to adaptively overweigh their prior information and underweigh the evidence implied by a dataset, particularly if the latter is perceived to be noisy or unreliable~\cite{lin2022data .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Such intuitive thinking is often regarded as untrustworthy and problematic~\cite{kahneman1982judgment, gilovich2002heuristics .}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, recent research has highlighted the role of heuristics in promoting accurate decisions~\cite{albrechtsen2009can, gigerenzer2008heuristics .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Intuitive decision-making can sometimes lead to better outcomes than rational, analytical reasoning~\cite{dane2012should, sadler2004intuitive , particularly in high-risk high-uncertainty situations~\cite{huang2018role .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Non-statistically rational thinking could, thus, be useful when viewing a visualization with high uncertainty or `extreme' data.
elements.sent-2411-023: In essence, human inference-making can serve as a valuable complement to a strictly rational agent.
elements.sent-2411-024: In this study, we investigate situations where human visual inferences might outperform those of an ideal AI machine in accurately characterizing data-generating processes under varying uncertainty and sample conditions.
elements.sent-2411-025: Rather than assuming that inferences from an AI are always ideal, we compare the benefits and drawbacks of both sides, evaluating conditions in which the strength of one can compensate for the weakness of the other.
elements.sent-2411-026: To achieve this objective, we employ Bayesian models to assess how both humans and AI machines make inferences on datasets.
elements.sent-2411-027: Bayesian models offer a principled framework for incorporating prior beliefs and updating them based on observed evidence, making them suitable for analyzing complex datasets and assessing decision-making processes.
elements.sent-2411-028: Previous research has explored the application of Bayesian cognition approaches to data interpretation through visualization \cite{kim2019bayesian, kim2020bayesian where they evaluated the effectiveness of visualization techniques based on the alignment between human interpretations and the Bayesian model's conclusions.}
elements.sent-2411-029: While this method can be helpful in assessing overall effectiveness, relying solely on a model as a reliable reference may not always be ideal.
elements.sent-2411-030: In certain contexts, deviations from AI's rationality can be advantageous.
elements.sent-2411-031: For instance, a model might perceive a minor positive correlation between two unrelated variables, such as the number of ice cream cones sold and the number of shark attacks.
elements.sent-2411-032: In contrast, a human analyst might become more skeptical upon observing the same data, irrationally reinforcing their belief in no correlation, eventually avoiding a false-positive conclusion.
elements.sent-2411-033: This perspective acknowledges that human heuristics, though often simplistic and seemingly inferior~\cite{tversky1974judgment , can lead to more accurate, ecological inferences~\cite{gigerenzer2009homo .}}
elements.sent-2411-034: By characterizing factors that lead analysts to exceed machine-inference performance, we lay the foundation for designing human-in-the-loop systems capable of leveraging both the intuition of analysts and the computational power of machines to harness insights from visualizations.
elements.sent-2411-035: These systems could integrate analyst intuition, such as through natural language queries, into their decision-making processes, enhancing their ability to provide meaningful insights and recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-036: To explore the opportunities, we ask the following research questions.
elements.sent-2411-037: RQ1: How do human visual analysts compare to Bayesian agents that see that same data? We specifically compare the visual inferences of analysts to Bayesians: one informed with the prior knowledge of the human analyst, and another with a uniform prior that provides no preexisting knowledge before exposure to the data.
elements.sent-2411-038: RQ2: How do characteristics of data samples affect visual inference accuracy relative to Bayesian agents? We address these questions empirically in two crowdsourced studies.
elements.sent-2411-039: Specifically, we assess how accurately individuals perceive the true bi-variate relationship between pairs of attributes, upon being exposed to (potentially noisy) samples.
elements.sent-2411-040: We collect responses from viewers both before and after exposure to visualizations, allowing us to capture both their prior beliefs and posterior inferences about the data-generating process.
elements.sent-2411-041: We then measure the accuracy of human-vs-Bayesian inferences under different sample configurations, ranging from large samples that closely conform to the true parameters to small and potentially extreme samples.
elements.sent-2411-042: The work presented in this paper is a preliminary version of the research described in Koonchanok et al.~\cite{koonchanok2024trust , where we provide a more detailed analysis and additional experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-043: We address our research questions empirically in a crowdsourced study where we assess how accurately individuals perceive the true bi-variate relationship between pairs of attributes when exposed to (potentially noisy) samples.
elements.sent-2411-044: We collect responses from viewers both before and after exposure to visualizations, allowing us to capture both their prior beliefs and post-sample (posterior) inferences about the data-generating process.
elements.sent-2411-045: We then measure the accuracy of human-vs-Bayesian inferences under different sample extremeness, ranging from samples that closely conform to the true parameter to the extreme, potentially misleading samples that arise sporadically.
elements.figure-2411-001: [] \center \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth] {figures/elicitation_2.pdf} \caption{Elicitation Method. Participants utilize the first slider to adjust the correlation line (left). They then use the second slider to adjust the uncertainty range (right). Data points on the plot are refreshed at the frequency of 5 Hz to allow participants to see the model implication. } \label{fig:elicitation}
elements.sent-2411-046: We capture participants' prior and posterior beliefs through a graphical inference method, as illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:elicitation .}
elements.sent-2411-047: Previous research has shown that eliciting priors can be beneficial in multiple aspects \cite{koonchanok2021data, koonchanok2023visual, kim2017explaining, choi2019concept, heyer2020pushing, choi2019visual .}
elements.sent-2411-048: This interface enables participants to articulate two key parameters regarding their beliefs: the most likely true correlation coefficient between the two variables ($\mu$) and the associated uncertainty ($\sigma$).
elements.sent-2411-049: Participants enter their (prior or posterior) beliefs about these two parameters using two sliders.
elements.sent-2411-050: The first prompts them to indicate the ``most likely relationship'' along a continuum from `negative' to `positive' correlation.
elements.sent-2411-051: The second slider prompts participants to express their ``confidence level'' in the relationship, ranging from `highly uncertain' to moderately uncertain to `highly certain'.
elements.sent-2411-052: These two parameters are then used to populate the following model: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} y_i = \beta_0 + \beta x_i + \epsilon_i \label{eq:model} \beta \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2) \beta_0 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_b^2) \epsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, {\sigma_e^2}) \end{aligned}\end{equation} Where $\mu \in (-1, 1)$ is the expected slope of the relationship as specified by the relationship slider, and $\sigma \in (0, 0.6)$ is the uncertainty in the slope, specified in the second confidence slider.
elements.sent-2411-053: We also display a hypothetical outcome plot (HOP), during which a new set of data points is refreshed every 200 milliseconds.
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{tabular}{c|l|c} Correlation Question Ground Truth Positive What is the relationship between teachers' experience $\mu= 0.502$ and the average standardized test score for students? $\sigma= 0.172$ Negative What is the relationship between time spent on social $\mu= -0.422$ media and hours of sleep at night? $\sigma=0.146 $ No relationship What is the relationship between the length of people's $\mu= 0.030$ first name and last name? $\sigma=0.236 $ \end{tabular} \caption{Examples from the prompt questions used in our experiments. We collected a mean crowdsourced ground truth of the $\mu$ to determine both the correlation of and consensus level of each question.} \label{tab:example_questions}
elements.sent-2411-054: To provide plausible stimuli for the study, we developed a set of 40 prompt questions and corresponding ground truth models.
elements.sent-2411-055: The prompt questions covered a variety of common knowledge topics (see Table~\ref{tab:example_questions for examples), with bivariate relationships ranging from negative correlations to positive correlations.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Additionally, we also included attributes with no plausible relationship.
elements.sent-2411-057: The corresponding ground truth models for these prompts followed the same form as Equation~\ref{eq:model .}
elements.sent-2411-058: To initialize these models with plausible parameters rooted in common wisdom, we employed crowd workers recruited through Amazon Mechanical Turk.
elements.sent-2411-059: Workers ($n=61$) were prompted to respond to each of the 40 questions, using the model elicitation interface in \ref{fig:elicitation to provide their belief on the most likely relationship slope and their uncertainty around that relationship.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Responses from the workers were then averaged forming the two ground truth parameters for each question.
elements.sent-2411-061: We then selected 24 prompt questions, based on the topic variety, to be used in the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-062: These questions comprised six ground truth models with a positive relationship ($\mu>0$), six questions with a negative relationship ($\mu<0$), and 12 with no correlation ($\mu\approx0$).
elements.sent-2411-063: Individuals often rely on social knowledge when forming their beliefs.
elements.sent-2411-064: Social consensus (whether perceived or actual) can also serve as a tool to reduce uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-065: Therefore, we expect the agreement around the ground truth to impact people's inferences from visualizations.
elements.sent-2411-066: To quantify the latter, we measure the consistency of the crowd wisdom: Prompt questions exhibiting smaller variations in the elicited crowd beliefs are considered to reflect a higher degree of social consensus.
elements.sent-2411-067: This was determined based on the standard deviation of the elicited $\mu$ among the workers.
elements.sent-2411-068: Within each category (positive, negative, or no relationship), we designate half the question with the lowest standard deviation as `high' consensus, with the other half considered `low' consensus, representing lower agreement between workers on what the data-generating process should be.
elements.sent-2411-069: For each stimulus, we display the prompt question and ask the participant to provide their prior belief about the topic.
elements.sent-2411-070: The participant specifies their two prior knowledge parameters ($\mu$ and $\sigma$) using the belief elicitation device.
elements.sent-2411-071: We then expose participants to a random data sample generated from a ground truth model.
elements.sent-2411-072: Lastly, we ask them to provide a posterior inference using the same elicitation device as before.
elements.sent-2411-073: To understand how sample characteristics affect inference accuracy, we varied two sample properties: size and extremeness.
elements.sent-2411-074: The number of observations in a sample is an important factor for an analyst to consider when making inferences.
elements.sent-2411-075: Larger sample sizes generally supply stronger evidence about the underlying data-generating sample.
elements.sent-2411-076: We thus varied the size of samples shown to participants, using 7, 15, and 30 data points to represent `small', `medium', and `large' sample sizes, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-077: These sample sizes were selected to allow for varying levels of evidence, while still allowing for extreme samples to emerge.
elements.sent-2411-078: Specifically, smaller samples are more prone to noise, giving a potentially misleading picture of the underlying ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-079: A core question that we address in this work is how resilient people are to extreme (or spurious) samples, as compared with idealized statistical machines.
elements.sent-2411-080: Sample extremeness reflects the degree to which it is consistent with the ground truth model.
elements.sent-2411-081: Under a random sampling regime, spurious samples are expected to naturally arise in the study.
elements.sent-2411-082: Consequently, we do not explicitly manipulate sample extremeness.
elements.sent-2411-083: Instead, we simply quantify and record the extremeness of generated stimuli in every trial.
elements.sent-2411-084: Under the law of large numbers, we expect samples generated to conform to the ground truth on the aggregate.
elements.sent-2411-085: In particular, the majority of samples should reflect the expected correlation coefficient of the corresponding ground truth model.
elements.sent-2411-086: However, extreme and spurious would be expected to emerge in the experiment (e.g., a sample showing a positive correlation when the underlying ground truth indicates no relationship).
elements.sent-2411-087: We use Pearson's coefficient ($R$) to characterize the strength of correlation for a sample and, by extension, the degree to which it can be considered extreme with respect to its underlying ground truth model.
elements.sent-2411-088: Specifically, we compute a difference ($\Delta R$) between the sample's correlation and the expected strength of the correlation: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \Delta R = R_{Sample} - R_{Expected} \label{eq:extremeness} \end{aligned}\end{equation} Where $R_{Sample}$ is the sample's correlation coefficient and $R_{Expected}$ is the mean R-value, determined from 10,000 simulated draws from the ground truth model.
elements.sent-2411-089: A sample that is a faithful representation of the data-generating model will have $\Delta R \approx 0$.
elements.sent-2411-090: As $\Delta R$ deviates (either negatively or positively), the sample will be considered more extreme.
elements.sent-2411-091: Under the law of large numbers, we would expect the distribution of $\Delta R$ to be centered around zero, with the majority of samples exhibiting low extremeness.
elements.sent-2411-092: Furthermore, we would expect the sample size to affect the likelihood of extreme samples: a sample with a larger number of data points will exhibit a narrower $\Delta R$ distribution.
elements.sent-2411-093: Conversely, samples with fewer data points will exhibit a wider extremeness distribution.
elements.sent-2411-094: Data-generating models with high uncertainty ($\sigma$) will also exhibit a greater likelihood of extreme samples that deviate from the expected slope.
elements.sent-2411-095: By measuring sample extremeness during the study, we can observe participants' reactions to spurious samples when those arise.
elements.sent-2411-096: Furthermore, we can gauge the degree to which participants (and Bayesian agents) are resilient to those misleading samples, and whether either can recover the underlying ground truth parameters.
elements.sent-2411-097: The experiment aims to compare the inference accuracy between participants to ideal Bayesian agents.
elements.sent-2411-098: We specifically test the extent to which participants can infer the ground truth parameters as a function of sample size, sample extremeness, and the degree of social consensus (along with interactions of these factors).
elements.sent-2411-099: Participants saw prompt questions described in \S\ref{sec:questions , and were asked to predict the parameters of a bi-variate relationship model, namely, the expected slope $\mu$ and uncertainty $\sigma$.}
elements.sent-2411-100: In each stimulus, participants were asked to provide their prior beliefs through the graphical elicitation interface described earlier.
elements.sent-2411-101: Specifically, participants adjusted the two sliders in Figure~\ref{fig:elicitation , until the observed HOP sample matched their expectation of the relationship.}
elements.sent-2411-102: Following the prior specification, they were then shown a data sample drawn randomly from the ground truth model side-by-side with their prior belief.
elements.sent-2411-103: During this step, they were asked to indicate how reliable they believed the sample to be, providing their subjective rating via a slider.
elements.sent-2411-104: Upon inspecting the sample, participants were prompted to provide their updated (posterior) belief, which reflect their inference ``about the true relationship'' via the same elicitation device.
elements.sent-2411-105: We specifically instructed participants to re-adjust the two parameter sliders again after considering the sample.
elements.sent-2411-106: The entire steps are shown in Figure \ref{fig:steps .}
elements.sent-2411-107: We adopt a mixed design, investigating two independent factors (varied within subjects): Sample size (3 levels) $\times$ Social consensus (2 levels).
elements.sent-2411-108: Sample characteristics: We varied sample characteristics within-subject.
elements.sent-2411-109: Specifically, participants completed an equal number of 8 trials with each sample size (small, medium, large).
elements.sent-2411-110: Participants also saw an equal number of low and high-consensus prompts.
elements.sent-2411-111: Sample extremeness was included in our analysis as an explanatory variable.
elements.sent-2411-112: Extremeness was left to vary as a consequence of the random sampling process.
elements.sent-2411-113: Hence, while not controlled on a participant level, extremeness followed a predictable distribution and was used as one of the explanatory factors in our modeling and analysis of the results (see \S\ref{sec:sampleProperties for a discussion of this).}
elements.sent-2411-114: Inferences: Participants supplied four continuous responses in every trial: the expected slope ($\mu$) and uncertainty ($\sigma$) in the relationship, both before (i.e., prior belief) and after sample exposure (i.e., posterior inference).
elements.sent-2411-115: For every trial, we generated two comparable posterior inferences.
elements.sent-2411-116: The first ($\mu_{Bayes}$ and $\sigma_{Bayes}$) was derived through Bayesian inference using the participant-provided prior and the likelihood function implied by the same sample observed by the participant.
elements.sent-2411-117: This corresponds to an idealized agent equipped with identical prior knowledge as the participant.
elements.sent-2411-118: The second response, also a Bayesian agent, utilized a flat prior in conjunction with the same sample-implied likelihood, thus representing a `blank-slate' agent that exclusively learns the two parameters ($\mu_{Flat}$ and $\sigma_{Flat}$) purely from the sample.
elements.sent-2411-119: We measured the distance between the ground truth parameters (i.e., true $\mu$ and $\sigma$) and the posterior separately for each of the three agents (Participant, Bayesian, and Flat Bayesian).
elements.sent-2411-120: Potential participants were recruited through Prolific.
elements.sent-2411-122: We recruited workers who are US residents with a minimum task-approval rate of 98\%.
elements.sent-2411-123: Participants received a \$5 compensation upon completing the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-124: The study was approved by Indiana University's institutional review board.
elements.figure-2411-003: [] \center \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth] {figures/steps_singlechart.pdf} \caption{Steps per trial that each participant. participants start by specifying initial belie of $\mu$ and $\sigma$ using the two sliders. They then observe the data sample and compare it against their prediction. Subsequently, they specify their posterior beliefs using the same interface as the first step.} \label{fig:steps}
elements.sent-2411-125: Before starting the experiment, participants went through a series of tutorials explaining the goal of the study and explained how the graphical elicitation device worked.
elements.sent-2411-126: The instructions emphasized that the samples shown could be noisy, particularly when the sample size is small.
elements.sent-2411-127: Participants then completed a practice trial.
elements.sent-2411-128: After the tutorial, participants completed the main study which consisted of 24 trials, corresponding to the prompt questions developed in \S\ref{sec:questions .}
elements.sent-2411-129: In addition to the analyzed trails, we included four engagement checks inserted randomly within the trial stream.
elements.sent-2411-130: The engagement questions consisted of questions with the same setup as the rest but with different instructions asking participants to move sliders to a specific location (e.g. extreme right).
elements.sent-2411-131: In each trial, participants were first presented with a context (e.g., supposed we have data on US cities), and were then prompted to predict a linear relationship between two variables (e.g. what is the relationship between the unemployment rate and rate of affordable housing?) The participant was asked to visually provide their prior belief through the elicitation device described in \S\ref{sec:elicitation .}
elements.sent-2411-132: Next, the participants were presented with a sample drawn from the ground-truth model, shown side-by-side with their prior.
elements.sent-2411-133: Lastly, the participant was prompted to update their prior belief by re-adjusting the same set of two sliders.
elements.sent-2411-134: Figure~\ref{fig:steps illustrates the sequence of these steps for a trial.}
elements.sent-2411-135: To characterize the accuracy of posterior inferences, we a metric that measures the divergence between the posterior and the true values.
elements.sent-2411-136: For readability, we normalize to obtain relative maximum/minimum deviation based on the range of parameters dictated by the sliders ([-1, 1]).
elements.sent-2411-137: Specifically: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \Delta \mu = \frac{ \mu_{Human|Bayes|Flat} - \mu_{Truth} }{2} \end{aligned}\end{equation} The Human response is the posterior inference of the participant, whereas Bayes and Flat are the normative Bayesian inferences that are either based on the participant-supplied prior or an assumption of a flat prior, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-138: In the subsequent results, we multiplied the number by 100 to represent percentage values.
elements.sent-2411-139: We used the brms package \cite{burkner2017brms to fit the responses to a Bayesian regression model.}
elements.sent-2411-141: We modeled both the mean and variance of the divergence, although the mean is our primary interest.
elements.sent-2411-142: We started by constructing a simpler model formulation that included the primary experimental factors.
elements.sent-2411-143: We then followed a model-expansion approach, adding interaction effects to improve the model fit (assessed using posterior predictive simulations).
elements.sent-2411-144: We also added random effects to account for individual differences among participants. \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \Delta \mu \sim Normal(\text{mean}, \text{sd}^2) \text{mean} = \Delta R \times agent \times size \times consensus + \Delta R\times agent \times questionType + (1+\Delta R \times agent ~|~participant) + (1~|~question) log(\text{sd}) = agent \times size \times consensus + (1+size \times agent~|~participant) + (1|question) \end{aligned}\end{equation} $\Delta R$ is the sample extremeness (as defined in Equation~\ref{eq:extremeness ), agent indicates whether the inference came from a Human (the participant), a Bayesian, or flat-prior agent.}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation} \Delta \mu \sim Normal(mean, sd^2) mean = \Delta R \times agent \times size \times consensus + \Delta R\times agent \times questionType + (1+\Delta R \times agent ~|~participant) + (1~|~question) log(sd) = agent \times size \times consensus + (1+size \times agent~|~participant) + (1|question) \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-145: Size is a categorical variable representing the sample size (Small, Medium, or Large), consensus is the social consensus around the ground truth, and questionType is a categorical variable indicating whether the true model prescribes positive correlation, negative correlation, or no relationship.
elements.sent-2411-146: Participants completed the experiment in 16.39 minutes on average.
elements.sent-2411-148: We first compare how participants perform compared with the Bayesian machines.
elements.sent-2411-149: We then analyze how each experimental factors affect the inference accuracy.
elements.figure-2411-004: [] \center \includegraphics[width=0.5\linewidth] {figures/exp1_humanMachine_mu.pdf} \caption{Comparison between humans and statistical machines in inferring true $\mu$, contingent on sample extremeness. The top histogram illustrates the empirical distribution observed at various $\Delta R$ levels.} \label{fig:exp1_HumanMachine}
elements.sent-2411-150: Figure~\ref{fig:exp1_HumanMachine illustrates the relative resilience of humans compared to statistical inference.}
elements.sent-2411-151: Specifically, we depict the estimated difference in divergence between participants on one hand and informed Bayesian agents (blue) and uninformed Bayesian agents (grey) on the other.
elements.sent-2411-152: An estimate above 0 suggests superior inferential performance for humans compared to statistical machines, while a value below zero indicates the opposite.
elements.sent-2411-153: When $\Delta R=0$ (indicating a sample fully consistent with the ground truth), the model predicts that humans will underperform, showing a deficit of $-1.62$ (CI: [$-2.06, -1.14$]) against informed Bayesian agents and $-2.37$ (CI: [$-2.90, -1.84$]) relative to an uninformed, data-driven agent.
elements.sent-2411-154: At $\Delta R = 0.2$ , however, the model predicts nearly equal performance for humans and informed Bayesian agents and a substantial advantage for humans over an uninformed agent.
elements.sent-2411-155: The performance advantage for humans can be expected to further widen at $\Delta R = .4$, relative to informed Bayesian.
elements.subsection-2411-009: To what extent are humans resilient to misleading samples compared to Bayesian agents?
elements.figure-2411-005: [] \center \includegraphics[width=1 \linewidth]{figures/extremeness_short.pdf} \caption{The sensitivity of the three agents to sample extremeness. The regression line shows model estimates. Points depict the observed, empirical responses. A weaker correlation implies more resilience to spurious samples.} \label{fig:exp1_lineCharts}
elements.sent-2411-156: Figure \ref{fig:exp1_lineCharts -top illustrates the impact of sample extremeness on the divergence from the ground truth for the three agent types.}
elements.sent-2411-157: A weaker correlation (i.e., smaller slope) indicates lower sensitivity and, hence, better resilience to spurious samples.
elements.sent-2411-158: Participants appear to be less influenced by extreme samples than both informed and uninformed (flat prior) Bayesian agents.
elements.sent-2411-159: Specifically, a unit increase in sample extremeness leads to a 13.53 (95\% CI: [$11.43, 15.82$]) increase in divergence from the true relationship.
elements.sent-2411-160: By contrast, a Bayesian agent is influenced more strongly by misleading samples (18.61, CI: [$17.1, 20.16$]), leading to higher divergence at more extreme samples.
elements.sent-2411-161: As would be expected, an agent with a flat prior learning purely from the data is influenced the most (31.73, CI: [$30.96, 32.52$]), leading to a strong association between sample quality and inference accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-162: When there is more consensus around the ground truth, participants are more resilient to spurious samples (9.65, CI: [7.32, 12.11] for high consensus vs., 17.46 CI: [14.82, 20.29] for low consensus).
elements.sent-2411-163: Lastly, sample size also plays a role, with participants most skeptical of small samples (8.57, CI: [6.36, 10.91]), followed by medium (12.90, CI: [10.16, 15.74]) and large (19.21, CI: [15.79, 22.77]).
elements.sent-2411-164: For both Bayesian agents, their sensitivities to extremeness by social consensus and sample size closely align with the overall estimate.
elements.sent-2411-165: Overall, both informative and uninformative Bayesian machines are more accurate than participants when data samples closely represent the data-generating models.
elements.sent-2411-166: However, as samples become more extreme, participants become more accurate relative to the Bayesian machines.
elements.sent-2411-167: Participants eventually outperform the machines at certain points and continue to widen the gap as extremeness increases.
elements.sent-2411-168: Even though participants outperform the Bayesian machines for a wide range of extremeness, the machines are still more accurate overall because extreme samples are less likely to be generated.
elements.sent-2411-169: Our results reveal that individuals often deviate from normative inference while analyzing visualizations.
elements.sent-2411-170: Interestingly, these deviations can be beneficial, especially when viewing visualizations that potentially include extreme data from small sample sizes.
elements.sent-2411-171: In such cases, an analyst's intuition may afford more accurate inferences than those produced by an ideal inference machine, even if the machine is calibrated with the participants' pre-existing knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-172: Conversely, for larger and more reliable datasets, the precision offered by machine-based inferences can reduce bias, leading to more accurate inference.
elements.sent-2411-173: These findings suggest that humans and machines excel in different scenarios, highlighting the potential for collaboration between humans and AI-based agents in inference-making.
elements.sent-2411-174: This collaboration allows each to complement the limitations of the other~\cite{wang2020human, steyvers2022bayesian .}
elements.sent-2411-175: For instance, a system could offer feedback and recommendations on visual inference, taking into account the sample's characteristics and the user's prior knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-176: If the current sample seems reliable, factoring in aspects like sample size, then one might lean toward relying on a machine.
elements.sent-2411-177: Conversely, in cases where the data appears noisy, relying on human heuristics might prove more suitable.
elements.sent-2411-178: While including humans as part of a system has demonstrated benefits in several machine learning studies \cite{daee2018user, mozannar2022teaching, steyvers2022bayesian, wang2022interpretability, salimzadeh2023missing , the challenge in building such visual analytics systems is to determine how much trust to allocate to the AI and when to be skeptical.}
elements.sent-2411-179: One reason is that it is not straightforward to pre-determine factors such as sample extremeness when encountering new data.
elements.sent-2411-180: Furthermore, it might be a challenge for humans to accurately elicit prior knowledge that reflects statistical parameters.
elements.sent-2411-181: With the assistance of LLMs, we believe that the latter challenge could be addressed.
elements.sent-2411-182: Through natural language input, we have the potential to enhance precise knowledge elicitation, thereby bolstering the effectiveness of visual data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-183: In this study, we investigate whether human inference can outperform AI models when making inferences from graphical patterns.
elements.sent-2411-184: We conducted a crowdsourced study to compare the inference accuracy of humans against Bayesian agents, incorporating multiple factors such as sample size and sample extremeness.
elements.sent-2411-185: Participants externalized both their prior beliefs (i.e., before seeing data) and posterior beliefs (i.e., after data exposure) using graphical elicitation.
elements.sent-2411-186: Our results demonstrate that humans were more accurate at inferring the true correlation level when the visualization depicted extreme data samples.
elements.sent-2411-187: These results underscore the potential effectiveness of integrating human-AI workflows to enhance the quality of decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-188: This paper is based upon research supported by the National Science Foundation under award 1942429.. \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format.}

elements.sent-2411-190: A clear and well-documented \LaTeX\ document is presented as an article formatted for publication by ACM in a conference proceedings or journal publication.
elements.sent-2411-191: Based on the ``acmart'' document class, this article presents and explains many of the common variations, as well as many of the formatting elements an author may use in the preparation of the documentation of their work.
elements.sent-2411-193: ACM's consolidated article template, introduced in 2017, provides a consistent \LaTeX\ style for use across ACM publications, and incorporates accessibility and metadata-extraction functionality necessary for future Digital Library endeavors.
elements.sent-2411-194: Numerous ACM and SIG-specific \LaTeX\ templates have been examined, and their unique features incorporated into this single new template.
elements.sent-2411-195: If you are new to publishing with ACM, this document is a valuable guide to the process of preparing your work for publication.
elements.sent-2411-196: If you have published with ACM before, this document provides insight and instruction into more recent changes to the article template.
elements.sent-2411-197: The ``\verb|acmart|'' document class can be used to prepare articles for any ACM publication --- conference or journal, and for any stage of publication, from review to final ``camera-ready'' copy, to the author's own version, with { very few changes to the source.}
elements.sent-2411-198: As noted in the introduction, the ``\verb|acmart|'' document class can be used to prepare many different kinds of documentation --- a dual-anonymous initial submission of a full-length technical paper, a two-page SIGGRAPH Emerging Technologies abstract, a ``camera-ready'' journal article, a SIGCHI Extended Abstract, and more --- all by selecting the appropriate { template style and { template parameters .}}
elements.sent-2411-199: This document will explain the major features of the document class.
elements.sent-2411-200: For further information, the { \LaTeX\ User's Guide is available from \url{https://www.acm.org/publications/proceedings-template .}}
elements.sent-2411-201: The primary parameter given to the ``\verb|acmart|'' document class is the { template style which corresponds to the kind of publication or SIG publishing the work.}
elements.sent-2411-202: This parameter is enclosed in square brackets and is a part of the {\verb|documentclass| command:.}
elements.sent-2411-203: Journals use one of three template styles.
elements.sent-2411-204: All but three ACM journals use the {\verb|acmsmall| template style:.}
elements.sent-2411-205: The majority of conference proceedings documentation will use the {\verb|acmconf| template style.}
elements.sent-2411-206: In addition to specifying the { template style to be used in formatting your work, there are a number of { template parameters which modify some part of the applied template style.}}
elements.sent-2411-207: A complete list of these parameters can be found in the { \LaTeX\ User's Guide.}
elements.sent-2411-208: Frequently-used parameters, or combinations of parameters, include:.
elements.sent-2411-209: Anonymizes the work and includes line numbers.
elements.sent-2411-210: Use with the \verb|\acmSubmissionID| command to print the submission's unique ID on each page of the work.
elements.sent-2411-211: This document uses the following string as the first command in the source file:.
elements.sent-2411-212: Modifying the template --- including but not limited to: adjusting margins, typeface sizes, line spacing, paragraph and list definitions, and the use of the \verb|| command to manually adjust the vertical spacing between elements of your work --- is not allowed.
elements.sent-2411-213: The ``\verb|acmart|'' document class requires the use of the ``Libertine'' typeface family.
elements.sent-2411-214: Your \TeX\ installation should include this set of packages.
elements.sent-2411-215: Please do not substitute other typefaces.
elements.sent-2411-216: The ``\verb|lmodern|'' and ``\verb|ltimes|'' packages should not be used, as they will override the built-in typeface families.
elements.sent-2411-217: The title of your work should use capital letters appropriately - \url{https://capitalizemytitle.com/ has useful rules for capitalization.}
elements.sent-2411-218: Use the {\verb|title| command to define the title of your work.}
elements.sent-2411-219: If your work has a subtitle, define it with the {\verb|subtitle| command.}
elements.sent-2411-220: Do not insert line breaks in your title.
elements.sent-2411-221: If your title is lengthy, you must define a short version to be used in the page headers, to prevent overlapping text.
elements.sent-2411-222: The \verb|title| command has a ``short title'' parameter:.
elements.sent-2411-223: Each author must be defined separately for accurate metadata identification.
elements.sent-2411-224: Multiple authors may share one affiliation.
elements.sent-2411-225: Authors' names should not be abbreviated; use full first names wherever possible.
elements.sent-2411-226: Include authors' e-mail addresses whenever possible.
elements.sent-2411-227: Grouping authors' names or e-mail addresses, or providing an ``e-mail alias,'' as shown below, is not acceptable:.
elements.sent-2411-228: The \verb|authornote| and \verb|authornotemark| commands allow a note to apply to multiple authors --- for example, if the first two authors of an article contributed equally to the work.
elements.sent-2411-229: If your author list is lengthy, you must define a shortened version of the list of authors to be used in the page headers, to prevent overlapping text.
elements.sent-2411-230: The following command should be placed just after the last \verb|| definition:.
elements.sent-2411-231: Omitting this command will force the use of a concatenated list of all of the authors' names, which may result in overlapping text in the page headers.
elements.sent-2411-232: The article template's documentation, available at \url{https://www.acm.org/publications/proceedings-template , has a complete explanation of these commands and tips for their effective use.}
elements.sent-2411-233: Note that authors' addresses are mandatory for journal articles.
elements.sent-2411-234: Authors of any work published by ACM will need to complete a rights form.
elements.sent-2411-235: Depending on the kind of work, and the rights management choice made by the author, this may be copyright transfer, permission, license, or an OA (open access) agreement.
elements.sent-2411-236: Regardless of the rights management choice, the author will receive a copy of the completed rights form once it has been submitted.
elements.sent-2411-237: This form contains \LaTeX\ commands that must be copied into the source document.
elements.sent-2411-238: When the document source is compiled, these commands and their parameters add formatted text to several areas of the final document:.
elements.sent-2411-239: Rights information is unique to the work; if you are preparing several works for an event, make sure to use the correct set of commands with each of the works.
elements.sent-2411-240: The ACM Reference Format text is required for all articles over one page in length, and is optional for one-page articles (abstracts).
elements.sent-2411-241: Two elements of the ``acmart'' document class provide powerful taxonomic tools for you to help readers find your work in an online search.
elements.sent-2411-242: The ACM Computing Classification System --- \url{https://www.acm.org/publications/class-2012 --- is a set of classifiers and concepts that describe the computing discipline.}
elements.sent-2411-243: Authors can select entries from this classification system, via \url{https://dl.acm.org/ccs/ccs.cfm , and generate the commands to be included in the \LaTeX\ source.}
elements.sent-2411-244: User-defined keywords are a comma-separated list of words and phrases of the authors' choosing, providing a more flexible way of describing the research being presented.
elements.sent-2411-245: CCS concepts and user-defined keywords are required for for all articles over two pages in length, and are optional for one- and two-page articles (or abstracts).
elements.sent-2411-246: Your work should use standard \LaTeX\ sectioning commands: \verb|section|, \verb|subsection|, \verb|subsubsection|, and \verb|paragraph|.
elements.sent-2411-247: They should be numbered; do not remove the numbering from the commands.
elements.sent-2411-248: Simulating a sectioning command by setting the first word or words of a paragraph in boldface or italicized text is { not allowed.}
elements.sent-2411-249: The ``\verb|acmart|'' document class includes the ``\verb|booktabs|'' package --- \url{https://ctan.org/pkg/booktabs --- for preparing high-quality tables.}
elements.sent-2411-250: Table captions are placed { above the table.}
elements.sent-2411-251: Because tables cannot be split across pages, the best placement for them is typically the top of the page nearest their initial cite.
elements.sent-2411-252: To ensure this proper ``floating'' placement of tables, use the environment table to enclose the table's contents and the table caption.
elements.sent-2411-253: The contents of the table itself must go in the tabular environment, to be aligned properly in rows and columns, with the desired horizontal and vertical rules.
elements.sent-2411-254: Again, detailed instructions on tabular material are found in the \LaTeX\ User's Guide.
elements.sent-2411-255: Immediately following this sentence is the point at which Table~\ref{tab:freq is included in the input file; compare the placement of the table here with the table in the printed output of this document.}
elements.figure-2411-006: \caption{Frequency of Special Characters} \label{tab:freq} \begin{tabular}{ccl} Non-English or Math Frequency Comments \O 1 in 1,000 For Swedish names $\pi$ 1 in 5 Common in math \$ 4 in 5 Used in business $\Psi^2_1$ 1 in 40,000 Unexplained usage \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-256: To set a wider table, which takes up the whole width of the page's live area, use the environment table* to enclose the table's contents and the table caption.
elements.sent-2411-257: As with a single-column table, this wide table will ``float'' to a location deemed more desirable.
elements.sent-2411-258: Immediately following this sentence is the point at which Table~\ref{tab:commands is included in the input file; again, it is instructive to compare the placement of the table here with the table in the printed output of this document.}
elements.figure-2411-007: \caption{Some Typical Commands} \label{tab:commands} \begin{tabular}{ccl} Command A Number Comments \texttt{{\char'134}author} 100 Author \texttt{{\char'134}table} 300 For tables \texttt{{\char'134}table*} 400 For wider tables \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-259: Always use midrule to separate table header rows from data rows, and use it only for this purpose.
elements.sent-2411-260: This enables assistive technologies to recognise table headers and support their users in navigating tables more easily.
elements.sent-2411-261: You may want to display math equations in three distinct styles: inline, numbered or non-numbered display.
elements.sent-2411-262: Each of the three are discussed in the next sections.
elements.sent-2411-263: A formula that appears in the running text is called an inline or in-text formula.
elements.sent-2411-264: It is produced by the math environment, which can be invoked with the usual {\char'134begin\,\ldots{\char'134 end construction or with the short form \$\,\ldots\$.}}
elements.sent-2411-265: You can use any of the symbols and structures, from $\alpha$ to $\omega$, available in \LaTeX~\cite{Lamport:LaTeX ; this section will simply show a few examples of in-text equations in context.}
elements.sent-2411-266: Notice how this equation: \begin{math \lim_{n\rightarrow \infty x=0 \end{math , set here in in-line math style, looks slightly different when set in display style.. (See next section).}}}
elements.sent-2411-267: A numbered display equation---one set off by vertical space from the text and centered horizontally---is produced by the equation environment.
elements.sent-2411-268: An unnumbered display equation is produced by the displaymath environment.
elements.sent-2411-269: Again, in either environment, you can use any of the symbols and structures available in \LaTeX\@; this section will just give a couple of examples of display equations in context.
elements.sent-2411-270: First, consider the equation, shown as an inline equation above: \begin{equation}\lim_{n\rightarrow \infty}x=0\end{equation} Notice how it is formatted somewhat differently in the displaymath environment.
elements.sent-2411-271: Now, we'll enter an unnumbered equation: \begin{displaymath \sum_{i=0 ^{\infty x + 1 \end{displaymath and follow it with another numbered equation: \begin{equation}\sum_{i=0}^{\infty}x_i=\int_{0}^{\pi+2} f\end{equation} just to demonstrate \LaTeX's able handling of numbering.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-272: The ``\verb|figure|'' environment should be used for figures.
elements.sent-2411-273: One or more images can be placed within a figure.
elements.sent-2411-274: If your figure contains third-party material, you must clearly identify it as such, as shown in the example below.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{sample-franklin} \caption{1907 Franklin Model D roadster. Photograph by Harris \& Ewing, Inc. [Public domain], via Wikimedia Commons. (\url{https://goo.gl/VLCRBB}).} \Description{A woman and a girl in white dresses sit in an open car.}
elements.sent-2411-275: Your figures should contain a caption which describes the figure to the reader.
elements.sent-2411-276: Figure captions are placed { below the figure.}
elements.sent-2411-277: Every figure should also have a figure description unless it is purely decorative.
elements.sent-2411-278: These descriptions convey what’s in the image to someone who cannot see it.
elements.sent-2411-279: They are also used by search engine crawlers for indexing images, and when images cannot be loaded.
elements.sent-2411-280: A figure description must be unformatted plain text less than 2000 characters long (including spaces).
elements.sent-2411-281: For figures that convey important and complex new information, a short text description may not be adequate.
elements.sent-2411-282: More complex alternative descriptions can be placed in an appendix and referenced in a short figure description.
elements.sent-2411-283: For example, provide a data table capturing the information in a bar chart, or a structured list representing a graph.
elements.sent-2411-284: For additional information regarding how best to write figure descriptions and why doing this is so important, please see \url{https://www.acm.org/publications/taps/describing-figures/ .}
elements.sent-2411-285: A ``teaser figure'' is an image, or set of images in one figure, that are placed after all author and affiliation information, and before the body of the article, spanning the page.
elements.sent-2411-286: If you wish to have such a figure in your article, place the command immediately before the \verb|| command:.
elements.sent-2411-287: The use of \BibTeX\ for the preparation and formatting of one's references is strongly recommended.
elements.sent-2411-288: Authors' names should be complete --- use full first names (``Donald E.
elements.sent-2411-290: Knuth'') --- and the salient identifying features of a reference should be included: title, year, volume, number, pages, article DOI, etc. The bibliography is included in your source document with these two commands, placed just before the \verb|.

elements.sent-2411-295: Basé sur la classe de document ``acmart'', ce l'article présente et explique de nombreuses variations courantes, ainsi que autant d'éléments de mise en forme qu'un auteur peut utiliser dans le préparation de la documentation de leur travail.
elements.sent-2411-296: Basierend auf der Dokumentenklasse ``acmart'', this Artikel präsentiert und erklärt auch viele der gängigen Variationen so viele der Formatierungselemente, die ein Autor in der verwenden darf Vorbereitung der Dokumentation ihrer Arbeit.
elements.sent-2411-297: Basado en la clase de documento ``acmart'', este artículo presenta y explica muchas de las variaciones comunes, así como tantos de los elementos de formato que un autor puede usar en el preparación de la documentación de su trabajo.
